is a [borough](/wiki/Borough_(New_Jersey) "Borough (New Jersey)") in [Passaic County](/wiki/Passaic_County,_New_Jersey "Passaic County, New Jersey"), in the [U.S. state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") of [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey"). As of the [2020 United States census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), the borough's population was 11,735,[[8]](#cite_note-Census2020-8)[[9]](#cite_note-LWD2020-9) a decrease of 493 (−4.0%) from the [2010 census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census") count of 12,228,[[18]](#cite_note-Census2010-18)[[19]](#cite_note-LWD2010-19) which in turn reflected a decrease of 168 (−1.4%) from the 12,396 counted in the [2000 census](/wiki/2000_United_States_census "2000 United States census").[[20]](#cite_note-20)

**Ringwood** is a [borough](/wiki/Borough_(New_Jersey) "Borough (New Jersey)") in [Passaic County](/wiki/Passaic_County,_New_Jersey "Passaic County, New Jersey"), in the [U.S. state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") of [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey"). As of the [2020 United States census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), the borough's population was 11,735,[[8]](#cite_note-Census2020-8)[[9]](#cite_note-LWD2020-9) a decrease of 493 (−4.0%) from the [2010 census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census") count of 12,228,[[18]](#cite_note-Census2010-18)[[19]](#cite_note-LWD2010-19) which in turn reflected a decrease of 168 (−1.4%) from the 12,396 counted in the [2000 census](/wiki/2000_United_States_census "2000 United States census").[[20]](#cite_note-20)

It is the home of [Ringwood State Park](/wiki/Ringwood_State_Park "Ringwood State Park") which contains the [New Jersey Botanical Garden at Skylands](/wiki/Skylands_(estate) "Skylands (estate)") (plus [Skylands Manor](/wiki/Skylands_Manor "Skylands Manor")), the Shepherd Lake Recreation Area and historic [Ringwood Manor](/wiki/Ringwood_Manor "Ringwood Manor").

The Borough of Ringwood was incorporated by an act of the [New Jersey Legislature](/wiki/New_Jersey_Legislature "New Jersey Legislature") on February 23, 1918, from a "portion of the [Township of Pompton](/wiki/Pompton_Township,_New_Jersey "Pompton Township, New Jersey")", as one of three boroughs formed from Pompton Township, joining [Bloomingdale](/wiki/Bloomingdale,_New_Jersey "Bloomingdale, New Jersey") and [Wanaque](/wiki/Wanaque,_New_Jersey "Wanaque, New Jersey"), based on the results of a referendum held on March 22, 1918.[[21]](#cite_note-Story-21) The first organizational meeting of the borough council took place in the existing Borough Hall on May 6, 1918. The borough was named for an iron mining company in the area.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ringwood,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The [Lenape](/wiki/Lenape "Lenape"), an [Algonquian language](/wiki/Algonquian_languages "Algonquian languages")-speaking tribe of [Native Americans](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") who occupied much of the mid-Atlantic coastal areas and the interior mountains including along the [Delaware River](/wiki/Delaware_River "Delaware River") resided in the area of present-day Ringwood when Europeans first entered the area. Some retreated to the mountains to escape colonial encroachment.

Colonists called the local band the Ramapough, and named the [Ramapo River](/wiki/Ramapo_River "Ramapo River") and other regional features after them. Their descendants and [Afro-Dutch](/wiki/Afro-Dutch "Afro-Dutch") migrants from New York were among the people who formed the [multiracial](/wiki/Multiracial "Multiracial") group known as the [Ramapough Mountain Indians](/wiki/Ramapough_Mountain_Indians "Ramapough Mountain Indians"), recognized in 1980 as the "Ramapough Lenape Nation" [Native American](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") tribe by the state of New Jersey, though the federal government has denied their application for formal recognition.[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)

Early in the 18th century, colonists discovered [iron](/wiki/Iron "Iron") in the area. The Ogden family built a [blast furnace](/wiki/Blast_furnace "Blast furnace") in Ringwood in 1742. By 1765, [Peter Hasenclever](/w/index.php?title=Peter_Hasenclever&action=edit&redlink=1 "Peter Hasenclever (page does not exist)") used Ringwood as the center of his ironmaking operations, which included 150,000 acres (610 km2) in New Jersey, [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)") and [Nova Scotia](/wiki/Nova_Scotia "Nova Scotia"). Iron mining was prominent in the area from the 18th century until the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression"), and iron shafts and pits, landfills and other elements still exist. The London, Roomy, Peters and Hope mines were all originally opened by Peter Hasenclever's London Company.[[25]](#cite_note-History-25)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2a/Plein_Air_Painters_at_Long_Pond%2C_Ringwood%2C_NJ.JPG/500px-Plein_Air_Painters_at_Long_Pond%2C_Ringwood%2C_NJ.JPG)](/wiki/File:Plein_Air_Painters_at_Long_Pond,_Ringwood,_NJ.JPG)

*[Plein air](/wiki/Plein_air "Plein air")* painters painting at [Long Pond](/wiki/Long_Pond_Ironworks_State_Park "Long Pond Ironworks State Park") in Ringwood, NJ.

A number of well-known ironmasters owned and lived at Ringwood Manor from the 1740s to the late 19th century. During the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"), [Robert Erskine](/wiki/Robert_Erskine_(inventor) "Robert Erskine (inventor)") managed ironmaking operations from Ringwood, and became [George Washington](/wiki/George_Washington "George Washington")'s first geographer and Surveyor-General, producing maps for the [Continental Army](/wiki/Continental_Army "Continental Army"). Washington visited the Manor House several times. Ringwood iron was used in the famous [Hudson River Chain](/wiki/Hudson_River_Chain "Hudson River Chain"), and for tools and hardware for the army. One of the Manor's last owners was [Abram S. Hewitt](/wiki/Abram_S._Hewitt "Abram S. Hewitt"), ironmaster, educator, lawyer, [U.S. Congressman](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress"), and [Mayor of New York City](/wiki/Mayor_of_New_York_City "Mayor of New York City"). The Manor is part of a [National Historic Landmark](/wiki/National_Historic_Landmark "National Historic Landmark") District.[[25]](#cite_note-History-25)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ringwood,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Geography")]

According to the [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), Ringwood had a total area of 28.49 square miles (73.8 km2), including 25.59 square miles (66.3 km2) of land and 2.91 square miles (7.5 km2) of water (10.20%).[[1]](#cite_note-CensusArea-1)[[2]](#cite_note-GR1-2)

[Unincorporated communities](/wiki/Local_government_in_New_Jersey#Unincorporated_communities "Local government in New Jersey"), localities and place names located partially or completely within the borough include Brushwood Pond, Cupsaw Lake, Skyline Lake, Conklintown, Erskine, Harrison Mountain Lake, Lake Erskine, Upper Erskine Lake, Monksville, Negro Pond, Sheppard Pond, Stonetown, Upper Lake and Weyble Pond.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

The borough borders [Bloomingdale](/wiki/Bloomingdale,_New_Jersey "Bloomingdale, New Jersey"), [Wanaque](/wiki/Wanaque,_New_Jersey "Wanaque, New Jersey") and [West Milford](/wiki/West_Milford,_New_Jersey "West Milford, New Jersey") in Passaic County; [Mahwah](/wiki/Mahwah,_New_Jersey "Mahwah, New Jersey") and [Oakland](/wiki/Oakland,_New_Jersey "Oakland, New Jersey") in [Bergen County](/wiki/Bergen_County,_New_Jersey "Bergen County, New Jersey"); [Tuxedo](/wiki/Tuxedo,_New_York "Tuxedo, New York") and [Warwick](/wiki/Warwick,_New_York "Warwick, New York") in [Orange County, New York](/wiki/Orange_County,_New_York "Orange County, New York"); and [Ramapo](/wiki/Ramapo,_New_York "Ramapo, New York") in [Rockland County, New York](/wiki/Rockland_County,_New_York "Rockland County, New York").[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)

The town is split by the [Wanaque Reservoir](/wiki/Wanaque_Reservoir "Wanaque Reservoir"), which provides water to urban areas in Northeastern New Jersey.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

### 2010 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ringwood,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2010 census")]

The [2010 United States census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census") counted 12,228 people, 4,182 households, and 3,413 families in the borough. The [population density](/wiki/Population_density "Population density") was 485.0 per square mile (187.3/km2). There were 4,331 housing units at an average density of 171.8 per square mile (66.3/km2). The racial makeup was 92.58% (11,321) [White](/wiki/White_(U.S._census) "White (U.S. census)"), 1.36% (166) [Black or African American](/wiki/Black_(U.S._census) "Black (U.S. census)"), 1.24% (152) [Native American](/wiki/Native_American_(U.S._census) "Native American (U.S. census)"), 1.74% (213) [Asian](/wiki/Asian_(U.S._census) "Asian (U.S. census)"), 0.02% (2) [Pacific Islander](/wiki/Pacific_Islander_(U.S._census) "Pacific Islander (U.S. census)"), 1.18% (144) from [other races](/wiki/Race_and_ethnicity_in_the_United_States_census "Race and ethnicity in the United States census"), and 1.88% (230) from two or more races. [Hispanic or Latino](/wiki/Hispanic_(U.S._census) "Hispanic (U.S. census)") of any race were 5.78% (707) of the population.[[18]](#cite_note-Census2010-18)

Of the 4,182 households, 37.9% had children under the age of 18; 70.8% were married couples living together; 7.3% had a female householder with no husband present and 18.4% were non-families. Of all households, 14.5% were made up of individuals and 5.1% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older. The average household size was 2.90 and the average family size was 3.23.[[18]](#cite_note-Census2010-18)

24.7% of the population were under the age of 18, 7.0% from 18 to 24, 23.7% from 25 to 44, 33.1% from 45 to 64, and 11.3% who were 65 years of age or older. The median age was 42.1 years. For every 100 females, the population had 100.3 males. For every 100 females ages 18 and older there were 97.8 males.[[18]](#cite_note-Census2010-18)

The Census Bureau's 2006–2010 [American Community Survey](/wiki/American_Community_Survey "American Community Survey") showed that (in 2010 [inflation-adjusted](/wiki/Inflation_adjustment "Inflation adjustment") dollars) [median household income](/wiki/Median_household_income "Median household income") was $109,139 (with a margin of error of +/− $8,896) and the median family income was $117,793 (+/− $9,712). Males had a median income of $70,086 (+/− $9,303) versus $54,397 (+/− $6,682) for females. The [per capita income](/wiki/Per_capita_income "Per capita income") for the borough was $39,931 (+/− $2,197). Estimates of families and population below the [poverty line](/wiki/Poverty_line "Poverty line") were not available.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

[Same-sex couples](/wiki/Domestic_partnership "Domestic partnership") headed 37 households in 2010, an increase from the 26 counted in 2000.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

### 2000 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ringwood,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2000 census")]

As of the [2000 United States census](/wiki/2000_United_States_census "2000 United States census")[[15]](#cite_note-GR2-15) there were 12,396 people, 4,108 households, and 3,446 families residing in the borough. The [population density](/wiki/Population_density "Population density") was 491.0 people per square mile (189.6 people/km2). There were 4,221 housing units at an average density of 167.2 per square mile (64.6/km2). The racial makeup of the borough was 93.87% [White](/wiki/White_(U.S._Census) "White (U.S. Census)"), 1.61% [African American](/wiki/African_American_(U.S._Census) "African American (U.S. Census)"), 1.44% [Native American](/wiki/Native_American_(U.S._Census) "Native American (U.S. Census)"), 1.19% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_(U.S._Census) "Asian (U.S. Census)"), 0.01% [Pacific Islander](/wiki/Pacific_Islander_(U.S._Census) "Pacific Islander (U.S. Census)"), 0.67% from [other races](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)"), and 1.21% from two or more races. 4.25% of the population were [Hispanic](/wiki/Hispanic_(U.S._Census) "Hispanic (U.S. Census)") or [Latino](/wiki/Latino_(U.S._Census) "Latino (U.S. Census)") of any race.[[35]](#cite_note-Census2000-35)[[36]](#cite_note-Census2000SF1-36)

There were 4,108 households, out of which 42.1% had children under the age of 18 living with them, 73.5% were [married couples](/wiki/Marriage "Marriage") living together, 7.8% had a female householder with no husband present, and 16.1% were non-families. 12.1% of all households were made up of individuals, and 3.6% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older. The average household size was 3.00 and the average family size was 3.28.[[35]](#cite_note-Census2000-35)[[36]](#cite_note-Census2000SF1-36)

In the borough the population was spread out, with 27.6% under the age of 18, 6.0% from 18 to 24, 30.7% from 25 to 44, 27.9% from 45 to 64, and 7.9% who were 65 years of age or older. The median age was 37 years. For every 100 females, there were 100.1 males. For every 100 females age 18 and over, there were 95.3 males.[[35]](#cite_note-Census2000-35)[[36]](#cite_note-Census2000SF1-36)

The median income for a household in the borough was $81,636, and the median income for a family was $85,108. Males had a median income of $60,097 versus $36,005 for females. The [per capita income](/wiki/Per_capita_income "Per capita income") for the borough was $31,341. 2.8% of the population and 2.0% of families were below the [poverty line](/wiki/Poverty_line "Poverty line"). Out of the total population, 3.9% of those under the age of 18 and 2.2% of those 65 and older were living below the poverty line.[[35]](#cite_note-Census2000-35)[[36]](#cite_note-Census2000SF1-36)

## Parks and recreation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ringwood,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Parks and recreation")]

[Ringwood State Park](/wiki/Ringwood_State_Park "Ringwood State Park") is a 4,444-acre (1,798 ha) state park located in the heart of the [Ramapo Mountains](/wiki/Ramapo_Mountains "Ramapo Mountains").[[39]](#cite_note-39) The Park consists of four distinct areas: Ringwood Manor, Skylands Manor/NJ State Botanical Garden, Shepherd Lake, and Bear Swamp Lake.

[Tranquility Ridge Park](/wiki/Tranquility_Ridge_Park "Tranquility Ridge Park") is a county park covering more than 2,000 acres (810 ha) of wooded land on the border of Ringwood and [West Milford](/wiki/West_Milford,_New_Jersey "West Milford, New Jersey") that was acquired by the county to preserve the property from development.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

The New Weis Center is an environmental education, arts and recreation center located at 150 Snake Den Road.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

Spring Lake Day Camp is an ACA-accredited summer day camp for children in Kindergarten through 10th grade.[[42]](#cite_note-42) The camp was founded in 1989 and has been family owned and operated since its opening.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

The [Highlands Natural Pool](/wiki/Highlands_Natural_Pool "Highlands Natural Pool") is an Olympic size, stream-fed freshwater pool that was carved and founded in 1935 by The Nature Friends, a group of residents who enjoyed working on recreational projects for the local community.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

## Law and government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ringwood,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Law and government")]

### Local government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ringwood,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Local government")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/Ringwood2.jpg/330px-Ringwood2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ringwood2.jpg)

Ringwood Manor, with a [mortar](/wiki/Mortar_(weapon) "Mortar (weapon)") and part of the [Hudson River Chain](/wiki/Hudson_River_Chain "Hudson River Chain")

Ringwood operates within the [Faulkner Act](/wiki/Faulkner_Act "Faulkner Act") (formally known as the Optional Municipal Charter Law) under the [Council-Manager](/wiki/Faulkner_Act_(council%E2%80%93manager) "Faulkner Act (council–manager)") form of municipal government Plan E, implemented based on the recommendations of a [Charter Study Commission](/wiki/Charter_Study_Commission "Charter Study Commission") as of January 1, 1979.[[45]](#cite_note-45) The borough is one of 71 municipalities (of the 564) statewide that use this form of government.[[46]](#cite_note-46) The borough's governing body is comprised of a seven-member borough council whose members are elected [at-large](/wiki/At-large "At-large") in partisan elections to serve four-year terms of office on a staggered basis, with either three or four seats coming up for election in odd-numbered years as part of the November general election.[[5]](#cite_note-DataBook-5)[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48) At an annual reorganization meeting held each January, the council selects a mayor and a deputy mayor from among its members.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

As of 2023[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Ringwood,_New_Jersey&action=edit), members of the Ringwood Borough Council are [Mayor](/wiki/Mayor "Mayor") Sean T. Noonan ([R](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)"), term on council ends December 31, 2025; term as mayor ends 2023), Deputy Mayor Jaime Matteo-Landis (R, term on council and as deputy mayor ends 2023), Stephanie N. Baumgartner (R, 2025), Stephanie A. Forest (R, 2025), Michelle Kerr (R, 2023; elected to serve an unexpired term), Linda M. Schaefer (R, 2023), and John M. Speer (R, 2023).[[3]](#cite_note-BoroughOfficials-3)[[50]](#cite_note-50)[[51]](#cite_note-PassaicDirectory-51)[[52]](#cite_note-Passaic2022-52)[[53]](#cite_note-Template2021-53)[[54]](#cite_note-Passaic2019-54)

In January 2022, the borough council appointed Michelle Kerr to fill the seat expiring in December 2023 that had been held by Michael McCracken until he resigned from office.[[55]](#cite_note-55) Kerr served on an interim basis until the November 2022 general election, when she was elected to serve the balance of the term of office.[[52]](#cite_note-Passaic2022-52)

#### Emergency services

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ringwood,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Emergency services")]

Ringwood is serviced by a volunteer [ambulance corps](/wiki/Emergency_medical_services "Emergency medical services") and three volunteer [fire companies](/wiki/Fire_company "Fire company"), with each fire company covering one section of the borough.[[56]](#cite_note-56) The Erskine Lakes Fire Company covers Erskine Lakes, and Cupsaw Lake.[[57]](#cite_note-57) Ringwood Volunteer Fire Company #1 (Stonetown) covers Stonetown.[[58]](#cite_note-58) and Skyline Lake Fire Department covers Skyline Lake area.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

### Highlands protection

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Template:NJhighlands&action=edit&section=T-1 "Edit section: Highlands protection")]

In 2004, the [New Jersey Legislature](/wiki/New_Jersey_Legislature "New Jersey Legislature") passed the [Highlands Water Protection and Planning Act](/wiki/Highlands_Water_Protection_and_Planning_Act "Highlands Water Protection and Planning Act"), which regulates the [New Jersey Highlands](/wiki/New_Jersey_Highlands "New Jersey Highlands") region. Ringwood was included in the highlands preservation area and is subject to the rules of the act and the [Highlands Water Protection and Planning Council](/wiki/Highlands_Water_Protection_and_Planning_Council "Highlands Water Protection and Planning Council"), a division of the [New Jersey Department of Environmental Protection](/wiki/New_Jersey_Department_of_Environmental_Protection "New Jersey Department of Environmental Protection").[[83]](#cite_note-83) All of the territory in the protected region is classified as being in the highlands preservation area, and thus subject to additional rules.[[84]](#cite_note-84)

### Politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ringwood,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Politics")]

As of March 2011, there were a total of 8,676 registered voters in Ringwood, of which 1,733 (20.0% vs. 31.0% countywide) were registered as [Democrats](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)"), 2,714 (31.3% vs. 18.7%) were registered as [Republicans](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") and 4,225 (48.7% vs. 50.3%) were registered as [Unaffiliated](/wiki/Unaffiliated_(New_Jersey) "Unaffiliated (New Jersey)"). There were 4 voters registered as [Libertarians](/wiki/Libertarian_Party_(United_States) "Libertarian Party (United States)") or [Greens](/wiki/Green_Party_(United_States) "Green Party (United States)").[[85]](#cite_note-VoterRegistration-85) Among the borough's 2010 Census population, 71.0% (vs. 53.2% in Passaic County) were registered to vote, including 94.3% of those ages 18 and over (vs. 70.8% countywide).[[85]](#cite_note-VoterRegistration-85)[[86]](#cite_note-86)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ringwood,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Education")]

Students in [kindergarten](/wiki/Kindergarten "Kindergarten") through [eighth grade](/wiki/Eighth_grade "Eighth grade") are served by the [Ringwood Public School District](/wiki/Ringwood_Public_School_District "Ringwood Public School District").[[103]](#cite_note-103) As of the 2023–24 school year, the district, comprised of four schools, had an enrollment of 1,019 students and 113.7 classroom teachers (on an [FTE](/wiki/Full-time_equivalent "Full-time equivalent") basis), for a [student–teacher ratio](/wiki/Student%E2%80%93teacher_ratio "Student–teacher ratio") of 9.0:1.[[104]](#cite_note-NCES-104) Schools in the district (with 2023–24 enrollment data from the [National Center for Education Statistics](/wiki/National_Center_for_Education_Statistics "National Center for Education Statistics")[[105]](#cite_note-105)) are
Peter Cooper Elementary School[[106]](#cite_note-106) with 234 students in grades K–3 (built in 1963),
Robert Erskine Elementary School[[107]](#cite_note-107) with 171 students in grades K–3 (built in 1960),
Eleanor G. Hewitt Intermediate School[[108]](#cite_note-108) with 228 students in grades 4–5 (built in 1937 with an annex built in 1952 and trailers added in 1959) and
Martin J. Ryerson Middle School[[109]](#cite_note-109) with 370 students in grades 6–8 (built in 1970).[[110]](#cite_note-110)[[111]](#cite_note-111)[[112]](#cite_note-112)

Ringwood's public schools are supported in part with grants from the Ringwood Educational Foundation, a not-for-profit organization which sponsors, among other things, the annual Shepherd Lake 5K run.[[113]](#cite_note-113)

Students in public school for [ninth](/wiki/Ninth_grade "Ninth grade") through [twelfth grades](/wiki/Twelfth_grade "Twelfth grade") attend [Lakeland Regional High School](/wiki/Lakeland_Regional_High_School "Lakeland Regional High School") in [Wanaque](/wiki/Wanaque,_New_Jersey "Wanaque, New Jersey"), which serves students from the Boroughs of Ringwood and Wanaque.[[114]](#cite_note-114) As of the 2023–24 school year, the high school had an enrollment of 847 students and 77.7 classroom teachers (on an [FTE](/wiki/Full-time_equivalent "Full-time equivalent") basis), for a [student–teacher ratio](/wiki/Student%E2%80%93teacher_ratio "Student–teacher ratio") of 10.9:1.[[115]](#cite_note-115)

Private schools used to include Ringwood Christian School, which was founded in 1973 through the Ringwood Baptist Church, serves 80 students in [kindergarten](/wiki/Kindergarten "Kindergarten") through [eighth grade](/wiki/Eighth_grade "Eighth grade"), with part-time sessions available for pre-schoolers.[[116]](#cite_note-116) St. Catherine of Bologna School, a regional [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholic "Roman Catholic") parochial school operating under the supervision of the [Roman Catholic Diocese of Paterson](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Diocese_of_Paterson "Roman Catholic Diocese of Paterson") that served kindergarten through eighth grade, closed in 2018 due to falling enrollment.[[117]](#cite_note-117)

## Community

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ringwood,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Community")]

Ringwood residents may be eligible to join one of several private lake communities, based on where they live: assorted lakes in Stonetown, Cupsaw Lake,[[118]](#cite_note-118) Erskine Lakes[[119]](#cite_note-119) or Skyline Lakes,[[120]](#cite_note-120) each of which have annual fees and initiation fees.[[121]](#cite_note-Living-121)

Each year on the third Saturday in March, Ringwood holds its annual [St. Patrick's Day](/wiki/St._Patrick%27s_Day "St. Patrick's Day") Parade, the only such parade in Passaic County.[[122]](#cite_note-122) Since 1990, the Parade Committee selects a grand marshal and a Citizen of the Year. These chosen outstanding citizens of the community are honored at a Unity Breakfast that precedes the parade. The parade includes bagpipe bands, floats, Irish step dancers, the county sheriff's department with their equestrian unit, local police, and fire and ambulance departments. Other marchers include Girl Scout and Boy Scout troops, local school groups and other recreational teams. The parade ends at the St. Catherine of Bologna Church Parish Center, where the celebration continues with live music and entertainment.

### Roads and highways

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ringwood,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Roads and highways")]

As of May 2010[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Ringwood,_New_Jersey&action=edit), the borough had a total of 87.52 miles (140.85 km) of roadways, of which 72.73 miles (117.05 km) were maintained by the municipality and 14.79 miles (23.80 km) by Passaic County.[[123]](#cite_note-123)

There are no state, U.S., or Interstate highways in Ringwood. The most prominent roads are [County Route 511](/wiki/County_Route_511_(New_Jersey) "County Route 511 (New Jersey)"),[[124]](#cite_note-124) which follows the Greenwood Lake Turnpike, and [County Route 692](/wiki/County_Route_692_(Passaic_County,_New_Jersey) "County Route 692 (Passaic County, New Jersey)"), which follows [Skyline Drive](/wiki/Skyline_Drive_(New_Jersey) "Skyline Drive (New Jersey)"). The nearest major highway is [Interstate 287](/wiki/Interstate_287 "Interstate 287"), and both CR 511 and CR 692 have interchanges with it in neighboring [Wanaque](/wiki/Wanaque,_New_Jersey "Wanaque, New Jersey") and [Oakland](/wiki/Oakland,_New_Jersey "Oakland, New Jersey"), respectively.[[125]](#cite_note-125) Ringwood had no traffic lights until June 2013, when the town's first one was installed at the intersection of Skyline Drive and Erskine Road. The borough still has no sidewalks or street lights.[[126]](#cite_note-126)

### Public transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ringwood,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Public transportation")]

[NJ Transit](/wiki/NJ_Transit "NJ Transit") bus transportation is available at the Ringwood Park and Ride, located adjacent to [Ringwood Public Library](http://www.ringwoodlibrary.org/). The [196](/wiki/196_(New_Jersey_bus) "196 (New Jersey bus)") offers express bus service to and from the [Port Authority Bus Terminal](/wiki/Port_Authority_Bus_Terminal "Port Authority Bus Terminal") in [Midtown Manhattan](/wiki/Midtown_Manhattan "Midtown Manhattan"), while the [197](/wiki/197_(New_Jersey_bus) "197 (New Jersey bus)") route offers local service, including to the [Willowbrook Mall](/wiki/Willowbrook_Mall_(Wayne,_New_Jersey) "Willowbrook Mall (Wayne, New Jersey)") and Willowbrook Park and Ride.[[127]](#cite_note-127)[[128]](#cite_note-128)

## Sister Cities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ringwood,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Sister Cities")]

* [![England](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/b/be/Flag_of_England.svg/40px-Flag_of_England.svg.png)](/wiki/England "England") [Ringwood, Hampshire](/wiki/Ringwood,_Hampshire "Ringwood, Hampshire"), England

The mayor of the borough of Ringwood, New Jersey approached Ringwood Town Council in September 1976, advising that the borough had, in recognition of the 750th anniversary of the Ringwood, Hampshire's market carter, resolved that the Hampshire town would become their Sister City. A laminated copy of the resolution was sent and several visits were subsequently exchanged on an official basis.[[141]](#cite_note-Official_Guide-141)
